name: 1947 Mobile Bears Baseball Team
summary: Brooklyn Dodgers Class AA farm team won Southern League title. Team members included Cal Abrams, Chuck “Rifleman” Connors and George “Shotgun” Shuba.

name: 1988 Vigor High School Football Team
summary: The only Alabama squad ever designated National High School Football Champions. The Wolves went 13-0 and allowed just 44 points all year as they won the Alabama 6A Championship. The team was named National High School Football Champions by ESPN. Eight players were named All-State. Several players went on to successful college and professional careers including Kevin Lee, Lectron Williams, Mitch Davis, Roosevelt Patterson, and Kelvin Sigler. The ’88 Vigor Wolves will join the 1947 Mobile Bears as the only teams inducted into the MSHF.

name: ALBERT “TIPPING” TERRY
summary: Tuskegee University Hall of Fame inductee. Successful high school football coach at Willliamson and Blount who led Williamson to the 1962 state title.

name: AMOS OTIS
summary: 17-Year Major League Baseball career with Royals, Mets and Pirates (1967-84). Five-time American League All-Star and Three-time Gold Glove winner.

name: AMY CHAPMAN KLEINSCHRODT
summary: Kleinschrodt is a five time gold medal winner of the U.S. Sailing Association’s Women’s National Championship.

name: ANGELO HARRIS
summary: Alabama High School Champion in the mile. All SEC and SEC record setter in Track and Cross Country at Alabama. 40 year Cross Country and Track Coach at McGill-Toolen High School. Coached 73 All-State Runners.

name: ANN SCHILLING
summary: Volleyball coach for Bayside Academy compiling 28 state titles.

name: ANTONIO LANG
summary: Star at Duke University and helped lead Blue Devils to National Championships in 1991 and 1992. Played six years in NBA. Led LeFlore H.S. to state title.

name: BECKY DICKINSON
summary: One of winningest volleyball coaches in Alabama High School history, winning 11 state titles as head coach at McGill-Toolen. Also won three state basketball titles.

name: BEN HARRIS
summary: Quarterback at Toulminville High School and Alabama State. Won four state championships at Blount High School during the 1990’s. NFL High School Coach of the year in 1998. Member of the AHSAA Hall of Fame.

name: BETH BARRY
summary: Star golfer at the University of South Alabama (1966-70) and four-time Alabama State Women’s Golf Champion. Longtime high school golf coach.

name: BILL DOOLEY
summary: All-SEC offensive lineman at Mississippi State. Served as head football coach at North Carolina, Virginia Tech and Wake Forest (1967-92) and won three ACC titles and 161 games.

name: BILL MARTIN
summary: UMS “Flea Circus” football star. Multi-sport amateur athletic star in Mobile.

name: BILL MENTON
summary: Star baseball player and coach at Spring Hill College. Mobile radio and television sportscaster for over 30 years.

name: BILL SHIPP
summary: Star tackle at the University of Alabama 1949-51. Played for NFL’s New York Giants and was also a star in Canadian Football League with for 11 seasons (1955-65).

name: BOBBY JACKSON
summary: Starred at quarterback and defensive back at University of Alabama (1956-58). Played for NFL’s Packers, Eagles and Bears and was member of Eagles’ 1960 NFL Champs.

name: BUDDY AYDELETTE
summary: Star offensive lineman at the University of Alabama (1976-79). Played in NFL with Packers and Steelers and was a three-time All-Star in the USFL.

name: CAPTAIN MUNNERLYN
summary: Captain Munnerlyn enjoyed a decade-long NFL career, playing one of the game’s most challenging positions: cornerback.

name: CARL BENTON
summary: Football and track Star at UCLA. Helped lead Bruins to 10-1 season and berth in Rose Bowl in 1947.

name: CARVEL “BAMA” ROWELL
summary: Carvel “B ama” Rowell played six-years in the major leagues, and posted a .275 lifetime batting average, in a career interrupted by World War Two.

name: CASEY CONVERSE
summary: One of the United States’ pre-eminent distance swimmers during the 1970s. He set a pair of NCAA records while swimming for the University of Alabama and swam in the 1976 Summer Olympic Games in Montreal. For the last 29 years, Converse has been the head swimming coach at the Air Force Academy.

name: CASIMIR P. NEWTON
summary: Supervisor of Health, Physical Education and Recreation for Mobile Public Schools (1947-78). Helped start Special Olympics program in Mobile in 1961.

name: CHARLES T. RHODES
summary: Football Star at Alabama State University. Head football coach and athletic director at Mobile County Training School, winning state title in 1966. High school administrator.

name: CHARLIE “HOME RUN” DUFFEE
summary: The first ever Mobile-born and Alabama-born major league baseball player. As a rookie in 1889 with the St. Louis Browns of the then major league American Association, Duffee finished third in the A.A. in home runs. He also played for the Columbus Solons, the Washington Senators, and the Cincinnati Reds before his promising career and life were cut short by tuberculosis. He died in 1894 at the age of 28.

name: CHRIS SAMUELS
summary: All-American offensive lineman at University of Alabama. Won Outland Trophy for best lineman in college football in 1999. Six-time Pro Bowl offensive lineman in the NFL.

name: CLEON JONES
summary: 14-Year Major League Baseball career with Mets and White Sox (1963-76). Starting left fielder for Mets’ 1969 World Champions.

name: CLIFF ELLIS
summary: NCAA basketball coach at four schools – South Alabama, Clemson, Auburn and Coastal Carolina (1975-2010). Has recorded over 500 career victories and is all-time winningest coach at USA and Clemson

name: CLIFTON McNEIL, JR.
summary: Two-sport star at Grambling University (1962-64). Ten-year NFL career as receiver with five teams (1964-73) and was member of Browns’ 1964 NFL Champs.

name: CURTIS HORTON, SR.
summary: All-Star running back at Tuskegee Institute. Longtime Mobile area high school football coach at Mobile County Training, McGill and Williamson.

name: DAMEYUNE CRAIG
summary: Quarterbacked Blount High School to two state championships before starring at Auburn where he led the Tigers to the 1997 SEC Championship Game. Craig later played for the Carolina Panthers and in NFL Europe before embarking on a coaching career that has fea- tured stops with the Miami Dolphins, L.S.U., Florida State, South Alabama, and Auburn.

name: DAN JENNINGS
summary: Coach, scout, and General Manager in Major League Baseball.

name: DANNY SHERIDAN
summary: Sports writer, anaylst, and commentator for countless events appearing on all major networks.

name: DAVE STAPLETON
summary: Baldwin County local, Dave Stapleton played baseball for South Alabama and the Boston Red Sox.

name: DENNIS G. SMITHERMAN
summary: Spring Hill College basketball star. Served as sports writer and sports editor for the Mobile Press-Register for 39 Years.

name: DON HULTZ
summary: A durable, hard nosed defender, Don Hultz enjoyed a 12-year career in the NFL.

name: DON STAINBROOK
summary: “Mr. Softball of Mobile.” Legendary 23-Year career as fast-pitch softball pitcher recording over 800 wins and 150 no-hitters.

name: DR. THOMAS P. ROSANDICH
summary: Rosandich was the founder of the world renowned United States Sports Academy and was an internationally recognized track and field coach and sports innovator.

name: E.B. PEEBLES
summary: Football Star at The Citadel. Served as Senior Bowl President and Mobile Arts and Sports Association Chairman. Longtime community sports leader.

name: EDDIE HOLMES
summary: Three-sport star at UMS-Wright and baseball player at Spring Hill College. Coached basketball and golf for 30 years at UMS-Wright, winning 12 state championships in boys golf.

name: EDDIE STANKY
summary: All-Star Major League Baseball player (1943-53) and manager. Served as head baseball coach at the University of South Alabama from (1969-79, ’81-83), taking team to five NCAA Tournaments.

name: EDMUND DYAS IV
summary: Member of National Collegiate Football Hall of Fame. All-American running back at Auburn University (1958-60) who finished fourth in voting for the 1960 Heisman Trophy.

name: EDWARD “ED” SCOTT, SR.
summary: Negro League baseball player and longtime major league scout, including 40 years with Red Sox (1961-90). Signed Hank Aaron to first major league contract.

name: ERICK WALDER
summary: Erick Walder attended Murphy High School and the University of Arkansas and is considered the greatest combination jumper in college track history. He still holds the collegiate outdoor long jump record with a leap of 8.53 meters – a record that has stood for 22 years. He is a medal winner at the Goodwill Games, the World Championships and the World Indoor Championships.

name: FRANK BOLLING
summary: 13-Year Major League Baseball career with Tigers and Braves (1954-66). Two-time All-Star second baseman and Gold Glove Winner in 1958.

name: FRANK HOWARD
summary: National Collegiate Football Hall of Fame Inductee. Head football coach at Clemson University for 30 seasons (1940-69) recording 7 conference titles and 165 victories.

name: FRED CARLEY
summary: SEC Track Champion at Auburn. Successful high school track and cross country coach in Alabama, winning 11 state titles at Murphy, and Florida.

name: GORDON SMITH, JR.
summary: Mobile-area amateur golf champion in 1930s and ’40s.

name: GUY SUMLIN
summary: Welterweight boxing champion.

name: HANFORD DIXON
summary: Star defensive back at Theodore High and Southern Miss. 1st round draft pick in 1981. Three-time Pro Bowl defensive back for Cleveland Browns.

name: HAROLD CLARK
summary: Head football coach at Vigor High School (1974-94) who recorded 160 wins. Won two state championships (1987 & ’88) and 1988 team was named national champions by ESPN and USA Today.

name: HENRY W. PAPA
summary: Soccer player, coach, administrator and international official for over 60 years. Played at Naval Academy and was inducted into National Soccer Hall of Fame in 1995.

name: HENRY “HANK” AARON
summary: National Baseball Hall of Fame Inductee – 1982. 23-Year MLB career with Braves and Brewers (1954-76). Set major league record with 755 career home runs. Member of Baseball’s All-Century Team.
bio:
In 1969, Sports Illustrated put Hank Aaron on its cover for the first time. The Atlanta Braves were challenging for the pennant and "Hammerin' Hank" was closing in one of the most revered marks in baseball. No, not Babe Ruth's record of 714 home runs, but 3,000 hits.

The author wrote that Aaron, in part because he'd played in small markets (Milwaukee then Atlanta), and because of his quiet demeanor, had failed to achieve the level of stardom accorded to lesser players. However, this wrong was being righted as Aaron chased down hit 3,000.

Mickey Mantle was quoted calling Aaron the "most underrated great hitter of all time." The story noted that, yes, Aaron would almost surely reach 3,000, but Ruth's home run mark? "Since he is now 35, it is doubtful that Aaron will stay around long enough to hit the 176 homers he needs to pass Ruth," the author wrote.

Henry Louis "Hank" Aaron was born in 1934 in the area of Mobile known as, "Down the Bay," one of eight children of Herbert and Estella Aaron. One of his siblings, younger brother Tommie, joined him in the majors with two stints with the Braves.

When Hank was a boy, the Aarons moved to Toulminville in a home his father built with scrap wood and whatever other supplies he could find. Aaron picked cotton as a child, and credited the labor with giving him the powerful hands that helped make him one of the most feared hitters in history.

"I tell a lot of people I was a vegetarian before they knew what a vegetarian was," Aaron told a reporter on the occasion of his 80th birthday. "We didn't eat meat but once every two or three weeks."

Aaron, batting cross-handed, was a terror on the playgrounds. As a young teen, he played with semi-pro black teams, including the Mobile Black Bears. Local scout Ed Scott saw great promise in young Henry, and in 1951, signed him with the Negro League Indianapolis Clowns.

In 1952, the Boston Braves purchased Aaron's playing rights from the Clowns. Aaron destroyed minor league pitching for two years and in 1954 the Braves—by then in Milwaukee—called him up. There was no learning curve for Hank Aaron. In his second year, he was chosen for the first of 21 consecutive All-Star games.

Because of the Braves' lackluster record in the last half of Aaron's career, it's easy to forget that "Hammerin' Hank" enjoyed winning seasons his first 13 years in the league. From a personal and team standpoint, Aaron's greatest season was probably 1957. He led the National League in home runs (44) and RBIs (132), hit .322, and was named the National League MVP.

Aaron was carried off the field after rocking an 11th inning homer late that season to clinch the Braves first league title since 1914. In the World Series, he batted .393 as the Braves beat the New York Yankees in seven games. The Braves made it back to the series the next year, but lost to the Yankees.

In 1959, the player so associated with hitting for power led the league with a stratospheric .355 batting average.

In 1966, the Braves moved from Milwaukee to Atlanta. The team's performance was lackluster, despite Aaron's consistently Herculean output.

In 1973, the home run chase began in earnest. Aaron, at 39, hit 40 homers that year, but finished the season one shy of Ruth's mark. The chase, which had become a national obsession, would have to wait until the next season. Sports Illustrated reported that Aaron received almost a million letters in 1973. While some of it was hate mail, bigots angry that a black man would break Ruth's record, most came from fans rooting for the quiet, dignified Aaron.

On opening day of the 1974 season, he tied the record with a blast against the Reds in Cincinnati. From that point on, every Aaron at-bat was a national event.

Four days later—against the Dodgers, and in Atlanta—Aaron connected on his historic shot. Two fans ran onto the field chasing the loping Aaron from second to third base. At home plate, his teammates hoisted the home run champ. His mother Estella raced onto the field and gave him a hard, long hug as Atlanta, and the nation, exulted. It was one of the most spine-tingling moments in the history of sports.

In 1975, Aaron returned to Milwaukee when the Braves traded him to the Brewers. He would hit his final 21 home runs—734 to 755—in the American League, his final dinger coming in 1976.

No player in baseball history has put up greater overall hitting numbers than Hank Aaron. Consider the following hitting categories, and Aaron's rank in each:

RBIs: First, with 2,297.

Extra-base hits: First, with 1,477

Total bases: First, with 6,856

Home Runs: Second, with 755

Hits: Fourth, with 3,771.

Runs: Fourth, with 2,174

Within months of his retirement as a player, Aaron was back with the Braves. As the team's director of player development, a position he held for years, Aaron was instrumental in turning the franchise from a cellar-dweller to a perennial playoff team.

Aaron was overwhelmingly voted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in 1982, his first year of eligibility. In 1997, the City of Mobile built Hank Aaron Stadium, the home for the minor league Mobile Bay Bears.

In 2007, Barry Bonds broke Aaron's record. However, Bonds' use of steroids during his career mars his record and many still consider Aaron the true holder of the career home run mark.

In 2010, the Bay Bears moved Aaron's childhood home to the stadium facility and opened the Hank Aaron Childhood Home & Museum. Joining Aaron for the grand opening were his old friend, Baseball Commissioner Bud Selig, and Hall of Famers Willie Mays, Bob Feller, Rickey Henderson, Bruce Sutter, Reggie Jackson and Ozzie Smith.

It seems impossible that's it's been more than 40 years since Aaron broke Ruth's record. In 2014, the Braves honored "Hammerin' Hank" by wearing a patch on their uniforms commemorating the record-breaking blast by Mobile's most famous citizen.

name: HERMAN MAISEL
summary: Successful high school head coach who won state championship as basketball coach at Murphy H.S. in 1955. Also won state title as head golf coach at Murphy.

name: IVAN MAISEL
summary: Ivan Maisel is a sports writer with decades of coverage of college football.

name: J. FINLEY McRAE
summary: Community business and sports leader. Organized group that financed the construction of Ladd Stadium in 1948 and bringing the Senior Bowl to Mobile in 1950.

name: JACK ROBERTSON
summary: Jack Robertson enjoyed more than four decades of success in his hometown of Bay Minette, leading the Faulkner State men’s basketball program into national junior college prominence.

name: JACK TILLMAN
summary: Welterweight Boxing Champion.

name: JAKE PEAVY
summary: All-Star, Cy Young, and World Series winning pitcher for the Padres, Giants, and Red Sox.

name: JAMES TAYLOR
summary: Taylor was named All-County and All-State after rushing for 3,200 yards and 16 touchdowns in just eight games at Citronelle High School. He went on to play for Bear Bryant at Alabama and was a member of the 1973 National Championship team. A member of the MOWA Choctaw tribe, Taylor is the first Native American inducted into the Mobile Sports Hall of Fame.

name: JAMES “JIM” TATE
summary: Girls and boys track and cross country coach at St. Paul’s Episcopal School. In 1997, led girls cross country team to a national high school-record 14th straight state title. Teams have won over 60 state titles during career.

name: JASON CAFFEY
summary: University of Alabama basketball player and 2-time NBA Champion as a member of the Chicago Bulls.

name: JERREL WILSON
summary: Jerrel Wilson is considered one of the best punters in professional football history.

name: JIM “JIMBO” MASON
summary: Nine-Year Major League Baseball career as shortstop with five teams (1971-79). Member of 1976 New York Yankees World Series team.

name: JIMMY GREEN
summary: Standout collegate golfer at Auburn University[/vc_column_text]

name: JOE BULLARD, JR.
summary: Five-sport star at UMS. Three-time All-South defensive back at Tulane University (1969-71). Still holds numerous school records for punt return yardage, average and TD’s.

name: JOE GOTTFRIED
summary: Joe Gottfried became Athletics Director at the University of South Alabama in 1981, after coaching basketball at Ashland College and Southern Illinois University.

name: JOEY JONES
summary: University of Alabama All-SEC receiver. Member of Alabama’s All-Decade Team of the 1980s. Played three seasons in USFL and one year in NFL. Currently the head football coach at the University of South Alabama.

name: JOHN MITCHELL
summary: First African-American varsity football player at University of Alabama (1971-72). 36-Year coaching career at collegiate and NFL levels including longtime tenure as DL coach with Steelers.

name: JOHN W. STEBER III
summary: Star offensive lineman at both Vanderbilt and Georgia Tech. Selected in the 1945 NFL Draft and played guard for the Washington Redskins (1946-50).

name: JOHNNY BROWN
summary: Mobile Metro Golf Champion known as “The King” of Mobile Golf. Star golfer at the University of South Alabama.

name: JOHNNY “J.D.” SHELWOOD
summary: Two-time Alabama State Championship winning coach for LeFlore High School.

name: JON LIEBER
summary: All-American at South Alabama. 2nd round draft pick 1992 Kansas City Royals. 14 seasons in MLB. Winning 131 career games. Finished 4th for the Cy Young Award in 2001.

name: JOSH DONALDSON
summary: Josh Donaldson is a Major League Baseball All Star, Silver Slugger, and MVP winning third baseman.

name: JUAN PIERRE
summary: In 2003, Pierre helped the Florida Marlins to a World Series victory.

name: JULES “STORMY” MUGNIER
summary: Outstanding all-round athlete who was a star basketball and baseball player at Spring Hill College. Drafted by Boston Celtics in 1952 NBA Draft. Nationally-ranked amateur tennis player.

name: JUNE BUCKHAULTS MAYSON
summary: Alabama Women’s Golf Champion. Former women’s head golf coach at the University of South Alabama.

name: KAREN MAYSON BAHNSEN
summary: Now in her fourth decade as head coach of the LSU women’s golf team, she was named SEC Coach of the Year in 1995. As a player, Bahnsen led the LSU women’s golf team and at McGill-Toolen High School, she led the Yellow Jackets to two state titles. Her mother June Buckholtz Mayson is an inductee of the Mobile Sports Hall of Fame.

name: KELLI OGDAN HILLIER
summary: Kelli Ogden starred in volleyball at the University of Georgia, and is one of the winningest high school volleyball coaches in Alabama history.

name: KENNY STABLER
summary: All-American QB at Alabama (1965-67). 17-Year NFL Career with Raiders, Oilers and Saints. NFL MVP in 1974 and led Raiders to Super Bowl victory in 1977.

name: LANCE JOHNSON
summary: All-Star baseball player in the Major Leagues and South Alabama Second Team All- American.

name: LAURETTA FREEMAN-HORN
summary: Starred at Williamson High School before embarking on a remarkable career at Auburn University. Freeman led Auburn to the 1990 Final Four and was the SEC Player of the Year and First Team All America in 1993. She won a gold medal for the U.S. at the 1994 Good- will Games. Freeman played professional basketball overseas and in the ABL. After retiring, she has been an assistant coach at Auburn and a head high school coach in Mobile (St. Paul’s).

name: LAWYER TILLMAN
summary: Lawyer Tillman prepped at LeFlore High where he was a wide receiver, safety, kicker and punter. He also played on the Rattlers basketball team.

name: LEON LETT
summary: Leon Lett won three Super Bowl Championship rings, and earned two Pro Bowl appearances during his NFL career.

name: LEROY “SATCHEL” PAIGE
summary: National Baseball Hall of Fame Inductee – 1971 (First Negro League player inducted). Legendary baseball career that spanned 41 Years (1926-66). Oldest rookie in Major League history at age 42 in 1948.

name: LEVI WASHINGTON
summary: Football and baseball star at Grambling University. Recorded over 300 wins as football coach at Williamson High in Mobile and McCall H.S. in Tallulah, La.

name: LEWIS BRASSELL
summary: Member of National Softball Hall of Fame. Commissioner of Mobile Metro Softball Association for 20 Years.

name: LIONEL “RED” NOONAN
summary: Stellar fullback and linebacker at the University Alabama (1945, ’47-49). President of the “A” Club. Football and track Star at Murphy High School.

name: LOYD SKODA
summary: Lloyd Skoda, a high school baseball for 39 years, retired with a record of 902-265. He coached future major leaguers Coco Duncan, P.J. Walters, and Josh Donaldson. He was inducted into the Alabama Baseball Association Hall of Fame in 2003.

name: LUIS GONZALEZ
summary: Luis Gonzalez will be forever remembered for his RBI single off possibly the best reliever in the history of baseball, Mariano Rivera, to give the Arizona Diamondbacks a game seven win over the New York Yankees in the 2001 World Series.

name: MABEL WALKER THORTON
summary: Member of 1948 United States Olympic team, competing in the 100-yard dash and 440-relay at games held in London, England. Also starred on Tuskegee Institute’s track team.

name: MARK BARRON
summary: Two time National Champion with the Crimson Tide and first round NFL pick in 2012.

name: MARYDYE McDOLE
summary: McDole is generally regarded as the greatest receiver in the history of Mississippi State football. He left MSU as the school’s all-time leading receiver in yards (2,214) and catches (116). McDole was a 2nd round draft pick by the Minnesota Vikings in the 1981 NFL Draft. He also played for the Calgary Stampeders in the CFL and the Memphis Showboats in the USFL.

name: MIKE FULLER
summary: Defensive back at Auburn University (1971-74). Also excelled as DB and kick returner in NFL for Bengals and Chargers (1975-82).

name: MIKE GOTTFRIED
summary: NCAA football head coach at four schools – Murray State, Cincinnati, Kansas and Pittsburgh (1978-89) and longtime ESPN College Football analyst and commentator. Founded national community outreach program, Team Focus.

name: MILTON BOLLING III
summary: Major League Baseball shortstop with Red Sox, Senators and Tigers (1952-58). Served as executive with Red Sox organization from 1961-95

name: MONIQUE ADAMS WILLIAMS
summary: Highschool, College, and Professional vollyball champion as well as member of Team USA.

name: NYLA SHEPHERD
summary: An All-American volleyball player at L.S.U, Nyla Shepherd led the Tigers to an unprecedented level of success.

name: OZZIE SMITH
summary: National Baseball Hall of Fame Inductee – 2002. 19-Year career as shortstop with Padres and Cardinals (1978-96). Won 13 Straight Gold Glove Awards and was 15-Time All-Star.

name: PAT GALLE
summary: Highly successful high school head football, track and cross country coach. Has coached at UMS-Wright for 37 years, leading boys and girls teams that have won 32 state titles.

name: PAT WHITE
summary: A two sport star athlete at Daphne High School, Pat White led the Trojans to two state baseball championships and earned All American honors. In football, he led Daphne to a state championship game and was a Mr. Football finalist.

name: PAUL CHRISTOPER HARRIS, SR.
summary: All-SEC football star at the University of Alabama – 1973-76. Helped lead Alabama to 1974 and 1975 SEC Championships and played for NFL’s Buccaneers and Vikings.

name: PAUL CRANE
summary: All-American football player at the University of Alabama (1962-64). Played for NFL’s New York Jets from 1965-72 and was starting center in historic Super Bowl III victory.

name: PEGGY MARTIN
summary: Winningest volleyball coach in NCAA Division II history, recording 33 straight winning seasons and 1,064 wins at Central Missouri State.

name: PETE MYERS
summary: Starred at Williamson High, Faulkner State, and Arkansas Little Rock. Drafted for the Chicago Bulls in 1986. Played 11 seasons in the NBA. Golden State Warriors Assistant Coach.

name: PHIL SAVAGE
summary: Football and baseball star at the University of the South who went on to career as collegiate coach and NFL coach and executive. Helped build Super Bowl Champion Ravens in 2000 and was G.M. of Cleveland Browns. Named General Manager of the Senior Bowl in 2012.

name: RALPH TAYLOR III
summary: Baseball star at Alabama State who played in Reds and Indians organizations. Successful high school coach at Blount, AHSAA official and major league baseball scout for Astros and Braves.

name: RANDY McGILLBERRY
summary: Record-setting pitcher at Satsuma H.S. and Louisiana Tech who played for Kansas City Royals’ 1977 and 1978 American League Division Champs.

name: RAY “BUDDY” LAUTEN
summary: Football and basketball Star at UMS. Record-setting basketball performer at Spring Hill College (1948-50) and Spring Hill College Hall of Fame inductee.

name: REA SCHUESSLER
summary: Rea Schuessler guided the Senior Bowl in its formative years, allowing it to become an integral part of the pro football landscape, with a multi million dollar economic impact on the City of Mobile.

name: REGGIE COPELAND, SR.
summary: Former Murphy H.S. basketball and football star. Longtime SEC basketball official who officiated NCAA Championship Game. Major proponent of sports in Mobile as member of Mobile City Council.

name: RICH CASTER
summary: All-American football and track star at Jackson State. Had 13-Year NFL career with Jets, Redskins, Oilers and Saints (1970-82).

name: RICHARD TODD
summary: Star quarterback at the University of Alabama (1973-75) who helped lead Crimson Tide to three SEC Titles. Ten-year NFL career with Jets and Saints (1976-85).

name: RICK TRICKETT
summary: Rick Trickett is one of the most accomplished coaches in college football history, serving in a four decade career at some of the top programs in the nation.

name: RILEY SMITH
summary: All-American QB at Alabama who led team to 1935 Rose Bowl Victory. Played for Washington Redskins and was second player ever selected in NFL Draft (1936).

name: ROBERT BRAZILE, JR.
summary: All-American football star at Jackson State who had stellar 10-year NFL career with Houston Oilers. Seven-time Pro Bowl linebacker and five-time All-Pro.

name: RODNEY HUDSON
summary: Rodney Hudson was a standout offensive lineman at the high school, college, and professional level.

name: ROY PATTON
summary: Soccer coach for the University of South Alabama men’s team.

name: SAM PETTAWAY
summary: A highly successful boys and girls track coach at Davidson High School from 1976-92. His boys’ teams won state championships in 1976, ’81, ’82, ’84, ’85, ’86, ’87. His girls’ teams won state championships in 1987 and ’88. Pettaway’s athletes earned more than forty All-American distinctions and many went on to successful college careers. Pettaway was also involved for many years with the City of Mobile Parks and Recreation Track program.

name: SCOTT HUNTER
summary: Record-setting QB at University of Alabama (1968-70). Enjoyed nine-year NFL Career with Packers, Falcons, Bills and Lions (1971-79).

name: STAN GALLE
summary: Former Major League Baseball player with Senators. Served as head baseball coach at Spring Hill College for 27 seasons and school’s field is named in his honor.

name: STEVE KITTRELL
summary: Head baseball coach at University of South Alabama (1984-2010). All-time winningest coach in school history with over 1,000 wins.

name: SUSIE REDMAN
summary: Played on LPGA Tour for 20 years, with career earnings of over $1 million. Was runner-up at the 1995 Nabisco Dinah Shore Tournament – one of the LPGA’s four major events. Played at Wright School in Mobile.

name: TED “DOUBLE DUTY” RADCLIFFE
summary: Legendary Negro League Baseball Star for 27 seasons (1928-64). Earned nickname by catching and pitching both games of a double-header in Negro League World Series.

name: TEE MARTIN
summary: Tamaurice “Tee” Martin won the 1998 National Championship playing quarterback for the Tennessee Volunteers.

name: TERRY ADAMS
summary: 11-Year Major League Baseball career as relief pitcher (1995-2005). Member of Red Sox 2004 World Series Champs.

name: TERRY CURTIS
summary: High school head football coach at Shaw, Murphy and UMS-Wright for 21 seasons. Has recorded over 200 victories and won four state titles at UMS (2001, ’02, 04 and ’08).

name: TERRY HOPPER
summary: NAIA Hall of Fame Inductee. Men’s golf coach at University of Mobile (1984-2009) winning national title in 1997.

name: TOMMIE AGEE
summary: 12-Year Major League Baseball career with five teams (1962-73). 1966 A.L. Rookie of the Year and member of Mets’ 1969 World Series Champs.

name: TOMMY AARON
summary: Tommy Aaron is the younger brother of Hank Aaron and member of the Braves organization in Major League Baseball.

name: TURNER WARD
summary: 12-Year Major League Baseball career with six teams (1990-2001). Currently the manager of the Class AA Mobile BayBears minor league team.

name: VINCE DOOLEY
summary: Member of National Collegiate Football Hall of Fame. Head football coach at University of Georgia for 25 seasons (1964-88) winning 201 games, six SEC Titles and the 1980 National Championship.

name: W.J. “PETE” MILNE
summary: 14-Year professional baseball career (1943-56) including three seasons with New York Giants (1948-50). Earned reputation as outstanding pinch-hitter in majors.

name: WILLIAM BOBBY ROBINSON
summary: Negro League Baseball star (1925-42). Outstanding third baseman known as the “Human Vacuum Cleaner.”

name: WILLIAM EARLE SMITH, JR.
summary: All-around athlete at the Naval Academy, serving as football team captain in 1956. Was also Navy’s top golfer in 1956 and played catcher on Midshipmen’s baseball team.

name: WILLIAM PERDUE
summary: Skeet and clay target marksman. International Skeet Shooting Champion.

name: WILLIAM “BILLY” COLEMAN
summary: Star QB at Southern Mississippi (1961-62) who helped lead USM to 1962 Small College National Title. Longtime high school head football coach with 148 career victories.

name: WILLIAM “BILL” JESSIE
summary: High school football coach at Central, Toulmanville and LeFlore high schools for 27 seasons, recording 145 victories. High School football official and AHSAA administrator for 35 years.

name: WILLIE ANDERSON
summary: All-SEC offensive lineman at Auburn University. Four-time Pro Bowl selection and three-time first-team All-Pro (2004, ’05 and ’06) for NFL’s Cincinnati Bengals and Baltimore Ravens from 1996-2008.

name: WILLIE McCOVEY
summary: National Baseball Hall of Fame Inductee (1986). 22-Year Major League Baseball career with Giants, Padres and A’s (1959-80). Six-time All-Star who hit 521 career home runs.
